judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 answered better: it caught both the cross-tenant leak and the exports.py nightly_export runtime break, while Report 2 caught only the leak and missed the exports.py regression.

report_1.evidence: `tenant_id` is passed in (and is populated from `current_tenant_id` via the new `GET /appointments/search` route in `backend/app/api.py`), but it is never referenced in the SQL or bound as a parameter. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query` in `backend/app/db.py`

report_2.evidence: has no `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause at all, even though `tenant_id` is passed into the function as an argument (from `get_appointment_search` in `backend/app/api.py:19-23`, resolved via the `current_tenant_id` auth dependency) and is even selected in the output columns — it's just never used to filter.

report_1.evidence: `backend/app/db.py` line 7 vs. `backend/app/exports.py` line 8 ... every invocation of `nightly_export`, for every tenant, will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`, breaking the nightly per-tenant CSV export entirely

the raw SQL interpolates `status.value` via f-string rather than binding it as a parameter (not currently exploitable, since FastAPI's `AppointmentStatus` enum validation constrains it before reaching this function

report_2.evidence: I don't consider the f-string interpolation of `status.value` into the SQL ... a blocking security bug by itself — `status` is typed as `AppointmentStatus`, a `str, Enum` in `backend/app/schemas.py`, and FastAPI validates/coerces the query parameter against that enum before the handler runs, so an attacker cannot inject arbitrary text through it today.
